formula: [Emphasized element] + [rest of the clause, sometimes with adjusted word order]
formula_visual: [NORMALLY-LATER ELEMENT] moved to the FRONT of the sentence, for emphasis
when_to_use: Use fronting to move an object, complement, or adverbial phrase to the FRONT of a sentence (out of its normal position) for dramatic or rhetorical emphasis — common in persuasive speech, storytelling, and formal writing, closely related to Week 12's Inversion from L2 but broader in scope.
when_to_use_ar: استخدم التقديم لنقل مفعول به أو مكمل أو عبارة ظرفية لبداية الجملة (خارج موضعها الطبيعي) للتأكيد الدرامي أو البلاغي — شائع في الخطابة والقص والكتابة الرسمية.
why_arabic_speakers_struggle: Advanced learners have internalized standard SVO word order so thoroughly that deliberately breaking it for rhetorical effect can feel 'wrong' even when it's a legitimate, native, stylistic choice — recognizing WHEN fronting is appropriate (for real emphasis) versus when it just sounds like a grammar mistake requires refined judgment, not a fixed rule.

examples.en: Never before have I seen such courage. (adverbial fronting + inversion)
examples.ar: أبدًا ما شفت شجاعة زي كده

examples.en: Happy she was not, despite the good news. (complement fronting)
examples.ar: سعيدة لم تكن، رغم الأخبار الجيدة (تأكيد على الحالة)

examples.en: Down came the rain, harder than ever. (adverbial + inverted verb-subject for dramatic effect)
examples.ar: نزلت المطر بشدة، أقوى من أي وقت

examples.en: Little did they know what awaited them. (fixed emphatic fronting expression)
examples.ar: لم يكونوا يعلمون ما ينتظرهم (تعبير تأكيدي ثابت)

common_errors.wrong: Never before I have seen such courage.
common_errors.correct: Never before have I seen such courage.
common_errors.explanation: When a negative/limiting adverbial is fronted, the auxiliary must invert with the subject ('have I'), just like L2's Inversion rule — fronting and inversion often work together.
After fronting an object, the main clause still follows normal grammar rules — no extra 'to' is needed before 'accept' here.
This specific dramatic-fronting pattern (direction/place adverb + inverted verb-subject) requires the verb before the subject — 'came the rain', not 'the rain came', for the emphatic literary effect.

practice_fill_blank.sentence: Rarely ___ (I/encounter) such dedication in my career.

practice_fill_blank.answer: I have never regretted / I've never regretted
practice_fill_blank.hint: Fronted object, normal word order follows.

practice_fill_blank.sentence: So confident ___ (be/she) that no one dared question her.
Little ___ (we/know) how much things would change.

quick_rule: Fronting moves any element (object, complement, adverbial) to sentence-start for emphasis, often triggering inversion (auxiliary-before-subject) when the fronted element is negative/limiting or a place/direction adverb in dramatic narrative style. Reserve this for genuine rhetorical impact, not everyday neutral speech.
quick_rule_ar: التقديم بينقل أي عنصر لبداية الجملة للتأكيد، وغالبًا بيحفز القلب (الفعل المساعد قبل الفاعل).
mnemonic: FRONTING = 'put it up FRONT' when it's the single most important thing you want the listener to notice first.
connect_to_speaking: Practice with your buddy: tell a short dramatic story (real or invented) using at least 2 fronted structures for emphasis — notice how it immediately makes your storytelling sound more literary and impactful.
